lines: There's wood enough within.
fullText: There's wood enough within.

lines: As wicked dew as e'er my mother brushed
With raven's feather from unwholesome fen
Drop on you both. A southwest blow on you
And blister you all o'er.
fullText: As wicked dew as e'er my mother brushed With raven's feather from unwholesome fen Drop on you both. A southwest blow on you And blister you all o'er.

lines: I must eat my dinner.
This island's mine by Sycorax, my mother,
Which thou tak'st from me. When thou cam'st first,
Thou strok'st me and made much of me, wouldst
give me
Water with berries in 't, and teach me how
To name the bigger light and how the less,
That burn by day and night. And then I loved thee,
And showed thee all the qualities o' th' isle,
The fresh springs, brine pits, barren place and
fertile.
Cursed be I that did so! All the charms
Of Sycorax, toads, beetles, bats, light on you,
For I am all the subjects that you have,
Which first was mine own king; and here you sty me
In this hard rock, whiles you do keep from me
The rest o' th' island.
fullText: I must eat my dinner. This island's mine by Sycorax, my mother, Which thou tak'st from me. When thou cam'st first, Thou strok'st me and made much of me, wouldst give me Water with berries in 't, and teach me how To name the bigger light and how the less, That burn by day and night. And then I loved thee, And showed thee all the qualities o' th' isle, The fresh springs, brine pits, barren place and fertile. Cursed be I that did so! All the charms Of Sycorax, toads, beetles, bats, light on you, For I am all the subjects that you have, Which first was mine own king; and here you sty me In this hard rock, whiles you do keep from me The rest o' th' island.

lines: O ho, O ho! Would 't had been done!
Thou didst prevent me. I had peopled else
This isle with Calibans.
fullText: O ho, O ho! Would 't had been done! Thou didst prevent me. I had peopled else This isle with Calibans.

lines: You taught me language, and my profit on 't
Is I know how to curse. The red plague rid you
For learning me your language!
fullText: You taught me language, and my profit on 't Is I know how to curse. The red plague rid you For learning me your language!

lines: No, pray thee.
It would control my dam's god, Setebos,
And make a vassal of him.
fullText: No, pray thee. It would control my dam's god, Setebos, And make a vassal of him.

lines: All the infections that the sun sucks up
From bogs, fens, flats, on Prosper fall and make him
By inchmeal a disease! His spirits hear me,
And yet I needs must curse. But they'll nor pinch,
Fright me with urchin-shows, pitch me i' th' mire,
Nor lead me like a firebrand in the dark
Out of my way, unless he bid 'em. But
For every trifle are they set upon me,
Sometimes like apes, that mow and chatter at me
And after bite me; then like hedgehogs, which
Lie tumbling in my barefoot way and mount
Their pricks at my footfall. Sometime am I
All wound with adders, who with cloven tongues
Do hiss me into madness. Lo, now, lo!
Here comes a spirit of his, and to torment me
For bringing wood in slowly. I'll fall flat.
Perchance he will not mind me.
fullText: All the infections that the sun sucks up From bogs, fens, flats, on Prosper fall and make him By inchmeal a disease! His spirits hear me, And yet I needs must curse. But they'll nor pinch, Fright me with urchin-shows, pitch me i' th' mire, Nor lead me like a firebrand in the dark Out of my way, unless he bid 'em. But For every trifle are they set upon me, Sometimes like apes, that mow and chatter at me And after bite me; then like hedgehogs, which Lie tumbling in my barefoot way and mount Their pricks at my footfall. Sometime am I All wound with adders, who with cloven tongues Do hiss me into madness. Lo, now, lo! Here comes a spirit of his, and to torment me For bringing wood in slowly. I'll fall flat. Perchance he will not mind me.

lines: Do not torment me! O!
fullText: Do not torment me! O!

lines: The spirit torments me. O!
fullText: The spirit torments me. O!

lines: Do not torment me, prithee. I'll bring my
wood home faster.
fullText: Do not torment me, prithee. I'll bring my wood home faster.

lines: Thou dost me yet but little hurt. Thou wilt
anon; I know it by thy trembling. Now Prosper
works upon thee.
fullText: Thou dost me yet but little hurt. Thou wilt anon; I know it by thy trembling. Now Prosper works upon thee.

lines: These be fine things, an if they be not
sprites. That's a brave god and bears celestial liquor.
I will kneel to him.
fullText: These be fine things, an if they be not sprites. That's a brave god and bears celestial liquor. I will kneel to him.

lines: I'll swear upon that bottle to be thy true
subject, for the liquor is not earthly.
fullText: I'll swear upon that bottle to be thy true subject, for the liquor is not earthly.

lines: Hast thou not dropped from heaven?
fullText: Hast thou not dropped from heaven?

lines: I have seen thee in her, and I do adore thee.
My mistress showed me thee, and thy dog, and thy
bush.
fullText: I have seen thee in her, and I do adore thee. My mistress showed me thee, and thy dog, and thy bush.

lines: I'll show thee every fertile inch o' th' island,
and I will kiss thy foot. I prithee, be my god.
fullText: I'll show thee every fertile inch o' th' island, and I will kiss thy foot. I prithee, be my god.

lines: I'll kiss thy foot. I'll swear myself thy subject.
fullText: I'll kiss thy foot. I'll swear myself thy subject.

lines: I'll show thee the best springs. I'll pluck thee berries.
I'll fish for thee and get thee wood enough.
A plague upon the tyrant that I serve.
I'll bear him no more sticks, but follow thee,
Thou wondrous man.
fullText: I'll show thee the best springs. I'll pluck thee berries. I'll fish for thee and get thee wood enough. A plague upon the tyrant that I serve. I'll bear him no more sticks, but follow thee, Thou wondrous man.

lines: I prithee, let me bring thee where crabs grow,
And I with my long nails will dig thee pignuts,
Show thee a jay's nest, and instruct thee how
To snare the nimble marmoset. I'll bring thee
To clustering filberts, and sometimes I'll get thee
Young scamels from the rock. Wilt thou go with me?
fullText: I prithee, let me bring thee where crabs grow, And I with my long nails will dig thee pignuts, Show thee a jay's nest, and instruct thee how To snare the nimble marmoset. I'll bring thee To clustering filberts, and sometimes I'll get thee Young scamels from the rock. Wilt thou go with me?

lines: How does thy Honor? Let me lick thy shoe. I'll
not serve him; he is not valiant.
fullText: How does thy Honor? Let me lick thy shoe. I'll not serve him; he is not valiant.

lines: Lo, how he mocks me! Wilt thou let him, my
lord?
fullText: Lo, how he mocks me! Wilt thou let him, my lord?

lines: Lo, lo again! Bite him to death, I prithee.
fullText: Lo, lo again! Bite him to death, I prithee.

lines: I thank my noble lord. Wilt thou be pleased
to harken once again to the suit I made to thee?
fullText: I thank my noble lord. Wilt thou be pleased to harken once again to the suit I made to thee?

lines: As I told thee before, I am subject
to a tyrant, a sorcerer, that by his cunning hath
cheated me of the island.
fullText: As I told thee before, I am subject to a tyrant, a sorcerer, that by his cunning hath cheated me of the island.

lines: Thou liest, thou jesting monkey,
thou. I would my valiant master would
destroy thee. I do not lie.
fullText: Thou liest, thou jesting monkey, thou. I would my valiant master would destroy thee. I do not lie.

lines: I say by sorcery he got this isle;
From me he got it. If thy Greatness will,
Revenge it on him, for I know thou dar'st,
But this thing dare not.
fullText: I say by sorcery he got this isle; From me he got it. If thy Greatness will, Revenge it on him, for I know thou dar'st, But this thing dare not.

lines: Thou shalt be lord of it, and I'll serve thee.
fullText: Thou shalt be lord of it, and I'll serve thee.

lines: Yea, yea, my lord. I'll yield him thee asleep,
Where thou mayst knock a nail into his head.
fullText: Yea, yea, my lord. I'll yield him thee asleep, Where thou mayst knock a nail into his head.

lines: What a pied ninny's this!--Thou scurvy patch!--
I do beseech thy Greatness, give him blows
And take his bottle from him. When that's gone,
He shall drink naught but brine, for I'll not show him
Where the quick freshes are.
fullText: What a pied ninny's this!--Thou scurvy patch!-- I do beseech thy Greatness, give him blows And take his bottle from him. When that's gone, He shall drink naught but brine, for I'll not show him Where the quick freshes are.

lines: Ha, ha, ha!
fullText: Ha, ha, ha!

lines: Beat him enough. After a little time
I'll beat him too.
fullText: Beat him enough. After a little time I'll beat him too.

lines: Why, as I told thee, 'tis a custom with him
I' th' afternoon to sleep. There thou mayst brain him,
Having first seized his books, or with a log
Batter his skull, or paunch him with a stake,
Or cut his weasand with thy knife. Remember
First to possess his books, for without them
He's but a sot, as I am, nor hath not
One spirit to command. They all do hate him
As rootedly as I. Burn but his books.
He has brave utensils--for so he calls them--
Which, when he has a house, he'll deck withal.
And that most deeply to consider is
The beauty of his daughter. He himself
Calls her a nonpareil. I never saw a woman
But only Sycorax my dam and she;
But she as far surpasseth Sycorax
As great'st does least.
fullText: Why, as I told thee, 'tis a custom with him I' th' afternoon to sleep. There thou mayst brain him, Having first seized his books, or with a log Batter his skull, or paunch him with a stake, Or cut his weasand with thy knife. Remember First to possess his books, for without them He's but a sot, as I am, nor hath not One spirit to command. They all do hate him As rootedly as I. Burn but his books. He has brave utensils--for so he calls them-- Which, when he has a house, he'll deck withal. And that most deeply to consider is The beauty of his daughter. He himself Calls her a nonpareil. I never saw a woman But only Sycorax my dam and she; But she as far surpasseth Sycorax As great'st does least.

lines: Ay, lord, she will become thy bed, I warrant,
And bring thee forth brave brood.
fullText: Ay, lord, she will become thy bed, I warrant, And bring thee forth brave brood.

lines: Within this half hour will he be asleep.
Wilt thou destroy him then?
fullText: Within this half hour will he be asleep. Wilt thou destroy him then?

lines: Thou mak'st me merry. I am full of pleasure.
Let us be jocund. Will you troll the catch
You taught me but whilere?
fullText: Thou mak'st me merry. I am full of pleasure. Let us be jocund. Will you troll the catch You taught me but whilere?

lines: That's not the tune.
fullText: That's not the tune.

lines: Art thou afeard?
fullText: Art thou afeard?

lines: Be not afeard. The isle is full of noises,
Sounds and sweet airs that give delight and hurt not.
Sometimes a thousand twangling instruments
Will hum about mine ears, and sometimes voices
That, if I then had waked after long sleep,
Will make me sleep again; and then, in dreaming,
The clouds methought would open, and show riches
Ready to drop upon me, that when I waked
I cried to dream again.
fullText: Be not afeard. The isle is full of noises, Sounds and sweet airs that give delight and hurt not. Sometimes a thousand twangling instruments Will hum about mine ears, and sometimes voices That, if I then had waked after long sleep, Will make me sleep again; and then, in dreaming, The clouds methought would open, and show riches Ready to drop upon me, that when I waked I cried to dream again.

lines: When Prospero is destroyed.
fullText: When Prospero is destroyed.

lines: Pray you, tread softly, that the blind mole
may not hear a footfall. We now are near his cell.
fullText: Pray you, tread softly, that the blind mole may not hear a footfall. We now are near his cell.

lines: Good my lord, give me thy favor still.
Be patient, for the prize I'll bring thee to
Shall hoodwink this mischance. Therefore speak
softly.
All's hushed as midnight yet.
fullText: Good my lord, give me thy favor still. Be patient, for the prize I'll bring thee to Shall hoodwink this mischance. Therefore speak softly. All's hushed as midnight yet.

lines: Prithee, my king, be quiet. Seest thou here,
This is the mouth o' th' cell. No noise, and enter.
Do that good mischief which may make this island
Thine own forever, and I, thy Caliban,
For aye thy foot-licker.
fullText: Prithee, my king, be quiet. Seest thou here, This is the mouth o' th' cell. No noise, and enter. Do that good mischief which may make this island Thine own forever, and I, thy Caliban, For aye thy foot-licker.

lines: Let it alone, thou fool. It is but trash.
fullText: Let it alone, thou fool. It is but trash.

lines: The dropsy drown this fool! What do you mean
To dote thus on such luggage? Let 't alone,
And do the murder first. If he awake,
From toe to crown he'll fill our skins with pinches,
Make us strange stuff.
fullText: The dropsy drown this fool! What do you mean To dote thus on such luggage? Let 't alone, And do the murder first. If he awake, From toe to crown he'll fill our skins with pinches, Make us strange stuff.

lines: I will have none on 't. We shall lose our time
And all be turned to barnacles or to apes
With foreheads villainous low.
fullText: I will have none on 't. We shall lose our time And all be turned to barnacles or to apes With foreheads villainous low.

lines: O Setebos, these be brave spirits indeed! How
fine my master is! I am afraid he will chastise me.
fullText: O Setebos, these be brave spirits indeed! How fine my master is! I am afraid he will chastise me.

lines: I shall be pinched to death.
fullText: I shall be pinched to death.

lines: Ay, that I will, and I'll be wise hereafter
And seek for grace. What a thrice-double ass
Was I to take this drunkard for a god,
And worship this dull fool!
fullText: Ay, that I will, and I'll be wise hereafter And seek for grace. What a thrice-double ass Was I to take this drunkard for a god, And worship this dull fool!
